segments.s230-g65: The segment begins with the proper name 'Tahmatan' as the subject, followed by the adverbial 'بپیش' (forward/toward) and the place/target 'اندرون' (into the inner ranks). 'حمله برد' is a light-verb construction: حمله (attack) + برد (past verb) literally 'carried an attack' = 'launched an attack'. Here برد is a past tense/light verb used poetically rather than the noun-sense 'victory'. Word order is typical classical Persian: subject + pre-verbal adverbials/place + verbal predicate at the end.
segments.s230-g66: 'عنان را' is the definite object (عنان = 'the reins') marked by the object particle را. 'برخش تگاور' here functions as the recipient: 'Barxš, the rider' (a personal name plus epithet). 'سپرد' is the past tense verb 'entrusted/handed over' and takes 'عنان را' as its object. Note the standard Persian order: object (marked with را) appears before the verb; the recipient follows the object.
segments.s230-g67: همی is a pre-verbal continuous/iterative particle meaning 'kept on' or 'ever/continually'. خون is the subject ('blood'), چکانید is the past tense verb 'dripped/sprinkled'. 'بر چرخ ماه' is a prepositional phrase 'upon the wheel/sky of the moon' (چرخ used poetically for the heavens/sky and ماه = moon). Word order places the verb after its subject and adverbial phrases, as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s230-g68: ستاره is the subject 'the stars'. نظاره is a nominal/poetic predicate meaning 'gaze' or 'observation' and here functions as 'were gazing' (the verb is nominalized). 'بر آن رزمگاه' is the prepositional complement 'upon that battlefield'. The construction is compact and typical of epic diction: subject + verbal noun/predicate + prepositional phrase.
segments.s230-g69: ز (poetic 'from') begins a causal/partitive phrase 'from so much...', with بس meaning 'so much' or 'very many'. گرد here is the noun 'dust' (common poetic usage). کز ('که' + 'ز') introduces the relative/subordinate clause 'that rose from the battlefield' and بردمید is the past verb 'was carried up/rose' (here used in the sense of 'rose up' or 'was taken up' — the sense 'carry off' fits the poetic usage). The sequence is a compact relative clause modifying the preceding measure phrase.
segments.s230-g70: چنان شد که is a fixed idiom 'it became so that / such was the state that...' followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. کس with the negative verb ندید yields 'no one saw' (گزاره with negation). روی هامون 'the surface of the plain' is the object of دید (here placed before the verb), and ندید is the past negative 'did not see'. Overall, the clause expresses result: visibility of the plain was lost.
segments.s230-g71: This segment lists causes introduced by ز ('from/because of'): 'بانگ سواران' = 'the cries of the horsemen' and 'زخم سنان' = 'the wounds (caused by) spears'. The coordinating و links the two parallel nominal phrases; the whole phrase functions as a causal/attributive preface to the conclusion in the next segment.
segments.s230-g72: نبود is the past negative 'there was not' (or 'was not to be seen'). ایچ پیدا literally 'any visible thing' (ایچ = 'anything / nothing' in negative contexts) combines with نبود to mean 'nothing was visible at all'. رکیب is the item that is not visible ('rider/companion' here: someone who would hold the reins). 'از عنان' ('from the reins') indicates location or vantage—i.e., 'no rider was visible at (or from) the reins.' The clause uses an archaic word order where the predicate (نبود) appears before some constituents.
segments.s230-g57: که‌گر is the conditional particle (poetic equivalent of اگر) introducing an if-clause. نامداران is a plural substantive meaning 'the renowned ones' and is the topic; ز ایران سپاه uses ز (poetic 'from/of') to mark provenance/possession so that ایران سپاه = 'the host/army of Iran.' Word order places the conditional first, then the subject phrase.
segments.s230-g58: هزیمت پذیرد is a light predicate: هزیمت 'rout/defeat' as the thing received and پذیرد (3rd-singular subjunctive/present) here means 'to undergo / be subjected to' (i.e. 'be routed'). ز توران سپاه parallels the earlier z + noun construction: 'by / from the host of Turan.' The clause completes the conditional begun by که‌گر.
segments.s230-g59: سرش را is the direct object: سر 'head' + enclitic ش 'his' + object marker را. ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from the body' (ز = 'from/of', تن = 'body'). برکنم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form used with performative/future force here ('I will pluck off'). در زمان is an adverbial 'in that moment/at that time.' The verb برکنم governs the object cluster.
segments.s230-g60: ز خونش is another prepositional phrase 'from his blood' (خون + enclitic ش). کنم is 1st-person singular of کردن 'to do/make' meaning 'I will make/cause.' جویهای 'streams' is plural (جوی + های) and روان is an adjective 'flowing,' so the noun phrase means 'flowing streams.' Word order is object/oblique (ز خونش) + verb (کنم) + object complement (جویهای روان).
segments.s230-g61: بدانست is past tense 'knew/realized' (3rd-singular narrative). لشکر is the subject 'the host/army' following the verb (common in epic word order). که introduces the content clause. او شیرخوست is a predicative phrase: او 'he' + شیرخوست (an epithet 'milk-nursling' / 'nursling') stating a characteristic.
segments.s230-g62: بچنگش is a prepositional/locative-like compound meaning 'into his grasp / in his claws' (ب- + چنگ + enclitic ش). سرین and گوزن are listed nouns ('a saddle' and 'a stag' respectively) that function as the objects of desire; آرزوست is a stative copular predicate 'is (a) desire' (3rd-singular). The verbless construction places the noun list before the copula, yielding 'a saddle and stag in his grasp were his desire.'
segments.s230-g63: همه سوی خاقان نهادند روی is an idiomatic sequence: همه 'all/everyone' + سوی 'toward' + خاقان 'the Khaghan' (directed object). نهادند (past, 3rd-plural) 'they set/placed' combines with روی 'face' to form the idiom روی نهادن به = 'to turn one's face toward' (i.e. 'to turn toward'). The verb comes after the directional phrase, as is usual in elevated/poetic word order.
segments.s230-g64: بنیزه شده is a participial/periphrastic construction: بنیزه 'with a spear' + شده (past participle of شدن) 'having become / being so' = 'having taken up a spear.' هر یکی 'each one' is the subject, and جنگ جوی is a compound noun (جنگ 'war/fight' + جوی 'seeker') meaning 'war-seeker' or 'warrior.' The overall sense is 'each, speared/armed, was a fighter.'
segments.s230-g137: ایا is an interrogative/rhetorical particle introducing a question. گم‌شده is a past-participle adjective ("lost") modifying بخت; Persian places the modifier before the noun. بختِ بیچارگان is a possessed construction meaning "the fortune of the unfortunate," with بیچارگان (the hapless) as the possessor.
segments.s230-g138: همه quantifies the following predicate ("all" / "everyone"). زار here is an adjective meaning "wretched/lamenting." و با درد links an accompanying circumstance: با + درد = "with pain/anguish." غمخوارگان is a plural agent noun "those who share or comfort in grief." The copular verb is omitted (elliptical), so the clause reads as a predicate: "all (are) wretched and, with pain, (are) the grief-companions."
segments.s230-g139: شما را topicalizes/addresses "you" (with را marking topic/emphasis). ز before رستم means "about/of/from Rostam" (here marking the source/topic of information). نبود آگهی is the negated existential: نبود (past of بودن) + آگهی ("news/information") = "there was no news/you had no information."
segments.s230-g140: مگر here functions as "unless" (rhetorical). مغزتان is "your brains/minds" with the possessive suffix -تان. از خرد uses از to mark the content/source "of wisdom/understanding." شد تهی is a light verb + adjective construction: شد ("became") + تهی ("empty/devoid"), meaning "became empty/devoid (of)."
segments.s230-g141: کجا introduces a rhetorical question "where/where is there." اژدها را places "the dragon" as a topical/definite object (را). ندارد + verbal clause expresses nonexistence of such a case: ندارد بمرد literally "does not have (that) it die," i.e. "there is no dragon that does not die." بمرد is an old/poetic verbal form expressing the clause 'that it die' or an optative sense "may it die," used here in a relative/claused sense.
segments.s230-g142: همی is an adverbial particle meaning "always/continually" placed before the verb. پیل is the subject "elephant." جوید is the present/simple verb "seeks/searches." بروز is a locative/temporal noun phrase "on the day/at the occasion," and نبرد is the noun complement "battle; fight" — together "seeks the day of battle."
segments.s230-g143: شما را topicalizes/addresses "you." سر از رزمِ من is an idiomatic expression: سر از X سیر شدن = "become sated/tired of X;" here "سر از رزم من" = "(your) head/interest from my battles." سیر نیست is the negated stative "is not sated/does not have enough," so the whole means "you have not grown weary/tired of my fighting."
segments.s230-g144: مرا here is the object/topic "to me / for me" (the entry is split in the word list but functions as مرا). هدیه جز ... نیست is an existential negative construction: "there is no gift except ..." جز introduces the sole exception. گرز و شمشیر are the only items named; نیست negates any other gift/existence, giving: "I have no gift but the mace and the sword."
segments.s230-g1: گهار گهانی is an adverbial poetic phrase meaning ‘suddenly/at one instant’; بدَان is the fused preposition + demonstrative (ب + دان) meaning ‘to that’ (compare modern به آن), and جایگاه is the noun 'place/spot'. Word order is slightly inverted and compressed for poetic rhythm: adverbial + directional phrase.
segments.s230-g2: گوی شیرفش is a proper-name or title phrase (a person or figure called ‘Goy Shirfash’); با is the preposition 'with', درفش is 'banner/standard', and سیاه is the adjective 'black' placed after the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s230-g3: برآشفت is a past tense verb meaning 'he became furious / was enraged'. چون here introduces the subordinate clause meaning 'when/as' (a common classical usage), ترگ is the noun 'rag/shred', رستم is the proper name, and بدید is the past-tense verb 'he saw'. The verb برآشفت precedes the clause for poetic inversion.
segments.s230-g4: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/cry'; چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as'; شیر is 'lion' and ژیان ('life') functions adjectivally as 'living' or 'alive' in this compound simile; برکشید is the past tense 'he lifted/raised' (here 'he uttered/let out' a cry). The simile (خروشی چو شیر ژیان) directly modifies the action expressed by برکشید.
segments.s230-g5: بدو is the dative 'to him' (classical Persian), گفت is the verb 'said' (here reported speech marker), من is the first‑person pronoun 'I', کین is the noun 'vengeance/hatred' used as the object, ترکان چین is a genitival phrase 'the Turks of Chin' (Turks associated with China). Word order places the speech verb and dative early, then the speaker and his object.
segments.s230-g6: بخواهم is a first‑person (present/subjunctive) form meaning 'I will/wish to (do)' and here marks intention/future action. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of', سگزی is an ethnic or place-name (here functioning as an object/agent of vengeance), بر + ین + دشت yields 'this plain' (بر = towards/on, ین as the archaic form of این 'this', دشت = plain), and کین repeats the idea of 'vengeance' as the goal — so the phrase expresses 'I will exact vengeance from the Sagzī on this very plain.'
segments.s230-g7: برانگیخت is a past tense verb 'he roused/raised' (here applied to an animal), اسپ is 'steed/horse' (object), and از میان سپاه is the prepositional phrase 'out from the middle of the host/army'. The verb preceding its noun is a poetic inversion; the whole unit describes the horse being set in motion out of the ranks.
segments.s230-g8: بیامد is the past tense 'he came', بر here functions as 'toward/to' in a motion phrase, پیلتن is a place-name (Pilten) and کینه‌خواه is an epithet 'the avenger' or 'vengeful', placed after the locative to qualify the arrival or the arriving figure. The sequence is a compact poetic description: he came to Pilten, characterized as the avenger.
segments.s230-g145: ز (poetic 'from') + فتراک (saddle-cloth) begins the clause with the source/thing from which an action is done. بگشاد is the past (preterite) of گشودن used here as 'loosed' or 'opened' and governs the following object پیچان کمند (a participial adjective + noun: 'twisting lasso'). Word order is source – verb – object, typical of Persian poetry placing the source first for emphasis.
segments.s230-g146: خم خام is a nominal phrase ('the slack/loop, lit. the curve, described as خام/untainted/raw') followed by در کوهه زین فگند, where در (in/into) + کوهه (mountain) gives the location/direction and زین (from the saddle) marks origin; فگند is a past-tense verb 'threw/cast'. The verb comes finally (typical older/poetic order), so the clause reads 'he threw the slack loop (خم خام) from the saddle into the mountain.'
segments.s230-g147: برانگیخت is a past tense verb 'roused/raised' with رخش ('his steed') as its object; the coordinating و links the next clause. برآمد خروش is an impersonal/inhabitual phrasing 'a roar arose/issued forth' (برآمد = 'arose/issued forth', خروش = 'roar'), with verb following the noun as often in classical verse.
segments.s230-g148: همی here is an emphatic/temporal particle ('then/just then' or 'indeed'); it precedes the object phrase اژدها را (dragon + definite object marker را). بدرید is a past-tense transitive verb 'tore/cut' and گوش ('ear') is the direct object. The object marker را flags اژدها as definite and topical.
segments.s230-g149: بهر سو (toward the side/direction) introduces a locative phrase; که is the relative/connector 'that/where' introducing the subordinate clause. خام here names the thing/person cast (lit. 'the kham' or 'the raw one'), اندر is the classical preposition 'into/within', and انداختی is the past verb 'you threw/cast'. The clause thus means 'toward the side into which you threw the kham.'
segments.s230-g150: زمین (the ground/earth) is the affected place; از دلیران ('of/from the brave') indicates the people affected, and بپرداختی is a past form of پرداختن used here in a causative/result sense ('you laid low / you caused to fall' in context). The verb follows its objects, a common order in poetic diction, so the clause reads 'you brought (many) of the brave to the ground.'
segments.s230-g151: هرانگه که ('whenever/at that moment that') introduces a temporal/subordinate clause. او is the subject 'he', مهتری را ('the chief' with object marker را) is the object taken, and ز زین ('from the saddle') gives the origin. Word order places the temporal introducer and relative که first, then the clause 'he [او] took the chief from the saddle.'
segments.s230-g152: ربودی is the past tense 'he seized/took' matching the earlier subject; بخم here is an obscure/archaised term (preserved in the text) functioning as part of the object phrase 'the loop/portion (of the) noose' — followed by کمند (lasso/snare). از کمین ('from the ambush') gives the source of the noose, with the verb at the front of the action in classical poetic order: 'he seized the noose's loop from its ambush.'
segments.s230-g153: بدین is an old fused locative meaning ‘به این’ (’at/to this’), introducing the scene; رزمگه is the locative noun ‘battlefield’. The phrase بر سرافراز طوس is a prepositional/relational phrase: بر here marks position ‘before/upon/in the presence of’ and attaches to the epithet + name سرافراز طوس (‘the lofty/proud Tūs’). Word order places the scene-setting locative first, then the relation to the person.
segments.s230-g154: بابر is the sentence subject (a personal name). اندر is a locative/adverbial preposition ‘in/among/at’ used here in front of the verb. افراختی is an archaic past-tense form ‘he raised/erected’ (3rd‑person), taking بوق و کوس (‘horn and drum’) as its direct objects; the conjunctor و links the two instruments.
segments.s230-g155: ببستی is an archaic past-form meaning ‘he bound/tied’ (here translated as ‘bound’). از ایران کسی is the agent phrase ‘someone from Iran’ placed after the verb (old Persian word order can place agent after the verb), and دست اوی is the object ‘his hand’ with the possessive enclitic اوی ‘his’. The sequence thus reads ‘someone from Iran bound his hand.’
segments.s230-g156: ز (poetic ز) marks origin ‘from’ and هامون is ‘the plain’. نهادی is an archaic/perfective verb form from نهاد ‘to place/put/lead’ (here ‘took/led’), followed by سوی ‘toward’. کوه روی is a compound meaning ‘the face/side of the mountain’ (کوه + روی ‘face’), so the whole phrase means ‘(he) led (him) from the plain toward the mountain’s face.’
segments.s230-g33: بینداختش is a past‑tense verb with the third‑person enclitic object -ش meaning “him” (literally “he threw him”). همچو is a simile marker meaning “like.” برگ درخت is a noun phrase “leaf of a tree” (برگ = leaf, درخت = tree) placed after the simile marker to complete the comparison.
segments.s230-g34: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause (“which/that …”). بر شاخ او is a prepositional phrase “upon its/he’s branch” (بر = on, شاخ = branch, او = his/its). بر زند is the verb phrase where زند is the poetic/older form of زدن “to strike/hit” and is placed after the preposition (a metrical inversion); باد سخت is the noun + adjective “strong wind.”
segments.s230-g35: نگونسار is an adjective meaning “mournful/woeful” used predicatively; کرد is the past‑tense causative verb “made/caused,” so نگونسار کرد آن درفش کبود = “he made that dark‑blue standard mournful.” The direct object آن درفش کبود follows the verb here due to poetic word order.
segments.s230-g36: تو is the second‑person subject “you.” گفتی in normal Persian would be the 2nd‑person past “you said,” and گهار گهانی appears as a poetic/compound noun phrase meaning something like “respite/occasion,” followed by نبود (“there was not”), so the clause means “you said there was no [respite/occasion].” Note that گهار/گهانی are rare/poetic and here form the thing whose non‑existence is asserted by نبود.
segments.s230-g37: بدیدند is the past‑tense third‑person plural “they saw”; گردان is the subject “troop/troops.” که introduces the content clause, and رستم چه کرد is an indirect question/order inversion meaning “what Rostam did” (چه = what; کرد = did). Together: “the troops saw what Rostam did.”
segments.s230-g38: چپ و راست is an adverbial phrase “left and right” meaning “on both sides/all around.” برخاست is the past verb “arose/sprang up.” گرد نبرد is a poetic nominal phrase “the circle/round of battle” or simply “battle around,” so the clause states that fighting rose up on the left and the right (i.e., on all sides). The word order is inverted for poetic meter (normally گرد نبرد برخاست).
segments.s230-g39: درفش همایون is the noun phrase “the auspicious/royal banner” (درفش = standard/banner, همایون = auspicious/imperial). ببردند is the past‑tense third‑person plural “they carried/took” (preverbal b‑ prefix is classical), and the coordinating و plus کوس (“and the drum”) links the next image. The object appears before the verb here because of the poem’s word order.
segments.s230-g40: بیامد is the past “came” or “sounded/was heard” (here of the drum). سرافراز is an adjective “triumphant/lofty,” and گودرز و طوس are the proper names “Goudarz and Tous.” The construction places the adjective before the names for a compact, elevated style: “the drum sounded; Goudarz and Tous [came] triumphant.”
segments.s230-g81: برآورد is a past-tense verb (classical narrative) meaning 'brought forth/raised' and appears before its subject here (verb–subject order is common in Ferdowsi). رستم is the subject (proper name). برانسان in the line functions syntactically as an adverbial/complement modifying where/against whom the cry was raised; it is ambiguous (it can be read as بر + انسان ‘upon/against men’) or, if read with the provided lemma, as a phrase involving a cloak/mantle (i.e. ‘with his mantle’). خروش is the direct object 'a cry/roar' placed at the end.
segments.s230-g82: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'for' / 'as'); گفتی here functions as a parenthetical past 'you said' (classical second-person past), though the lemma senses given do not list this verbal meaning; برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth' and is the main verb of the clause; زمانه is the grammatical subject 'the age / the times'; بجوش is a jussive/poetic form of جوشیدن ('to boil, seethe') used here as a predicative, 'seethed/was in agitation'.
segments.s230-g83: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech; گفت here is the past 'they said / it was said' (the provided lemma senses give conversational nouns rather than the verb, so the exact listed senses don't match the verbal use); کان is an archaic demonstrative introducing the following noun ('that' / 'there was' in classical usage) placed before پیل 'elephant' (noun); آن تخت عاج is a noun phrase with demonstrative آن before تخت 'throne' and adjective/attribute عاج 'ivory' following it (typical adjective-noun order in poetry).
segments.s230-g84: همان here is a demonstrative 'the same / that' introducing a series of items. The nouns یاره, افسر, طوق, تاج are given in a coordinate list joined by و; they are enumerated objects (companions/attendant(s) or possibly an item named 'یاره', then helmet, necklace/collar, and crown). The sequence is simply a poetic listing of royal paraphernalia.
segments.s230-g85: سپرهای is the plural 'shields' with the plural maker -های attached; چینی is a postpositive modifier here (in poetry adjectives often follow or are juxtaposed) and can mean 'porcelain/China' or 'Chinese' depending on context; و links to the next noun group پرده سرای, where پرده 'curtain' is followed by سرای — in classical Persian poetry the connective (ezafe) is often omitted and compounds or noun phrases can be formed by simple juxtaposition, so پرده سرای functions as a compound roughly 'curtain-house / curtained pavilion' (note: the provided lemma-senses for سرای do not match the locative/structural noun sense, so the lexical sense is irregular in the supplied list).
segments.s230-g86: همان again 'the same/that', followed by افسر 'helmet'. آلت چارپای is a genitive-type juxtaposition (poetic omission of ezafe): آلت 'implements/gear' of چارپای 'four-footed animal / beast of burden' — together 'the tack/gear of pack-beasts'. The sequence lists further items of equipment.
segments.s230-g87: بایران is a plural/collective form related to بایر 'barren/fallow lands' and here functions as the topic ('the wastelands' / 'the barren lands'); سزاوار is a predicate adjective 'befitting / appropriate for'; کیخسروست is a nominal predicate formed of the proper name کیخسرو plus the poetic contracted copula -ست (for است), meaning 'is Kay Khosrow' or more naturally 'belongs to Kay Khosrow' depending on sense—together the phrase says the wastelands are fitting for Kay Khosrow.
segments.s230-g88: که begins a clause of explanation ('for' / 'because' / 'that'); او is the third-person pronoun 'he'; در جهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the world' (در = in, جهان = world); شهریار is a nominal predicate 'sovereign/king'; نوست is a poetic contraction of نو + است ('is new/fresh' in literal sense) but in context functions idiomatically as a predicate with ست (contracted copula) and should be read as part of the nominal sentence 'he is (a) king/sovereign' — the adjective نو can carry a figurative force here (see commentary), and the verb/copula is the contracted -ست.
segments.s230-g129: چنو (an archaic/poetic variant of چون/چنان) introduces a conditional/temporal clause 'if/when'. سست گردد is a verbal phrase where گردد (from گشت) means 'become' and is used here in a subjunctive/optative sense 'becomes'/'should become'; پیاده شوم is the predicative complement + first‑person verb شوم (subjunctive/optative form of شدن) meaning 'I become / I shall be' — literal order is 'if/when becomes weak, I become on foot (i.e. I am dismounted)'.
segments.s230-g130: This is a coordinated second clause describing the speaker's consequent state. بخون here reads as a prepositional fusion (بـ + خون = 'with/in blood') rather than the imperative 'sing' (the listed senses do not include this prepositional use), and و simply links the two nouns. خوی آهار داده شوم is passive: خوی 'disposition/temper', آهار 'stiffening/firming (figurative: composure, hardening)', داده شوم is a passive construction 'I will be made given' = 'my disposition will be made/firmed'.
segments.s230-g131: یکی لشکرست این is a verbless/existential phrase: یکی 'one/a certain', لشکرست (لشکر + است) 'is an army/host', این 'this' — word order here places the predicate before the demonstrative and comparison follows: چو 'like/as' introduces the simile مور و ملخ 'ants and locusts'.
segments.s230-g132: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; two coordinated prepositional phrases با پیل و با پیلبانان 'with elephants and with elephant‑keepers/mahouts' modify or contrast the addressed party. The final word مچخ is dialectal/obscure and functions as a predicate/adjective describing that party; its precise meaning is uncertain (listed as obscure), so it's left as an opaque descriptive term here.
segments.s230-g133: همه پاک is a nominal phrase 'all (of us), pure/clean' (پاک as predicative adjective). در پیش خسرو is a locative/phrasing 'in the presence of the king' (در پیش = before/in the presence of), and بریم (برد#بر) is a first‑person plural jussive/intentional form meaning 'we will take/go' or 'let us go/bring' — here 'we will go/before the king'.
segments.s230-g134: ز is the poetic genitive/preposition 'from'. شگنان is a tribal/epic name or plural noun ('the Shaghnān' / 'destroyers' depending on reading) and چین here refers to 'China' (a geographic source). هدیه نو بریم employs هدیه نو 'a new gift/tribute' with بریم (same verb as above) 'we will bring/take' — the clause states bringing fresh tribute from those lands.
segments.s230-g135: و زان جایگه is 'and from that place' (زان = 'from that'), and برخروشید is a past/plural narrative verb 'they rose up/erupted' (often of people rising in anger or movement). و گفت is the quotative 'and (they) said' introducing direct speech; in epic Persian گفت commonly heads reported speech.
segments.s230-g136: که introduces the reported content 'that'. با روم و چین 'with Rome and China' names the partners; اهرمن (Ahriman) is the evil spirit/name invoked. باد here is the optative/implicative particle meaning 'may (it be)', and جفت 'pair/ally' is the noun complement: 'that Ahriman may be paired/ally with Rome and China' (a curse or wish of alliance with evil).
segments.s230-g17: درفش تهمتن is a noun phrase: درفش (standard) followed by the epithet/name تهمتن without an explicit ezafe (a common poetic omission), so it reads “the standard of Tahmatan.” میان گروه is a locative phrase: میان (in the middle/midst) + گروه (troop/band), meaning “amid the ranks.” Word order (noun phrase then locative) is typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s230-g18: بسان is a comparative preposition ‘like/as.’ درخت is the simile’s head noun ‘tree.’ از + بر form a compressed, somewhat archaic locative/ablative phrasing here: از (from/out of) + بر (on/upon) together indicate origin/location “from/on the …”; تیغ کوه is a genitival phrase where تیغ (literally “sword,” used metaphorically for a ridge or blade) is governed by کوه (mountain), so “the mountain’s blade/crest.” Overall: “like a tree on the mountain’s ridge.”
segments.s230-g19: همی is a habitual/progressive particle meaning “kept on / was continuously.” تاخت is the past verb ‘galloped/rushed’; the subject رستم follows it (verb–subject order is common in Persian poetry). پس او is locative/sequential ‘after/behind him’ (پس = after/behind + pronoun او). چو گرد is a simile ‘like dust’ where گرد is a noun meaning dust; together they describe what followed him.
segments.s230-g20: زمین (earth/ground) is the subject and لعل گشت is a predicative phrase: لعل (ruby/deep red) + گشت (past of گشتن/شدن ‘became’) → “the ground became ruby.” The conjunction و links a parallel clause with omitted copula: هوا لاژورد literally “(the) air (became) lapis-lazuli,” where لاژورد names the blue color. The copula is explicit in the first clause and omitted in the second by coordination (a common poetic device).
segments.s230-g21: گهار گهانی is an archaic/poetic adverbial phrase meaning ‘now and then / occasionally’ (reduplicative styling). بترسید is the past tense ‘he feared / became afraid’ (ب- prefix appears in many older forms). سخت is an intensifier here (‘very/terribly/strongly’). Together: “occasionally he was greatly afraid.”
segments.s230-g22: کزو is a relative/ablative particle ‘from which / because of which.’ بود is past copula ‘there was / was.’ برگشتن is the infinitive/verbal noun ‘returning / reversal’ used here as the predicate’s verbal idea. تاج و تخت is a coordinated nominal phrase ‘crown and throne’ (objects/what is returned/overturned). The clause therefore gives the feared consequence: “from which there was the returning/losing of crown and throne.”
segments.s230-g23: برآورد is a past verb here meaning ‘he produced/uttered / raised’ (not the modern ‘estimate’ sense). یک بانگ is the direct object ‘a cry / a shout.’ برسان کوس is a causative/performative phrase: برسان (bring/let be sounded) + کوس (war‑drum) — in context it means he had the drum sounded or gave the signal on the drum. The two verbs are coordinated actions: he cried out and had the drum sounded.
segments.s230-g24: که is a subordinating conjunction ‘that / so that’ introducing the clause reporting perception. بشنید is the past tense ‘he heard’ (the verb precedes subject in poetic word order). آواز گودرز و طوس is the object phrase ‘the call/voice of Goudarz and Tous’ (two proper names coordinated by و).
segments.s230-g121: برو is an imperative (2nd-person singular) meaning “go.” It is followed by the prepositional phrase با دلیران (“with the brave”) where با precedes the plural noun دلیران. سوی introduces a directional phrase (toward) and governs the noun phrase دست راست; راست is a postposed adjective modifying دست (the usual noun–adjective order in Persian), giving “the right hand/side.” The whole segment is a single imperative clause commanding movement toward the right with companions.
segments.s230-g122: نگه کن is another imperative (نگه as a noun/verb root “look/watch” plus کن = “do”), meaning “keep watch/look.” که introduces a subordinate clause (’that’/’to see that’), here functioning like “to see/so that” or simply introducing the content of the watching. پیران و هومان کجا ست is an embedded clause: پیران و هومان is the coordinated subject, کجا is the interrogative/locative “where,” and ست (archaic-stylistic form of است) is the 3rd-person copula placed at the end — standard poetic word order for questions/indirect questions in Persian.
segments.s230-g123: طهمتن (a vocative name) appears first as the addressee; نگر is an imperative “look!” or “behold!” that follows the vocative. پیش خاقان is a locative/relational phrase meaning “before / in the presence of the Khaghan” (پیش = before/in front of, خاقان = khagan/ruler). چین here is a poetic noun “fold(s)/wrinkle(s)” and in the line it links to the following image of the sky — i.e. the folds/warpings of the sky being noticeable before the king.
segments.s230-g124: همی is an archaic/poetic continuous particle (giving progressive/ongoing sense: “is/are …ing”). آسمان is the grammatical subject “the sky/heavens.” برزند is an archaic 3rd-person plural verb form meaning here “fall/come down” (poetic usage). بر is the preposition “on/upon/toward” and زمین is the object “earth,” so the clause reads literally “the heavens are falling upon the earth.” Note the older verb form and the fronted particle give a lofty, heightened style.
segments.s230-g125: برآشفت is a past-tense verb meaning “became angry/was enraged.” It appears before the subject (رهام), a common poetic V–S order. همچون پلنگ is a simile (“like a leopard”) modifying the manner of his anger. The sequence is straightforward: the verb of emotion, the person, then the comparative phrase.
segments.s230-g126: بیامد is a past-tense verb “(he) came.” بپشت here is a contracted poetic form of بهِ پشت / به پشت (“to the back of”); such contractions are common in classical Persian verse. تهمتن is the referent (Tahmatan). بجنگ is the verbal-prefix form (ب- + جنگ) used to express purpose or direction of action, here “to fight”/“for battle.” Together the clause means he came up to Tahmatan’s back in order to fight him.
segments.s230-g127: چنین گفت رستم is a conventional narrative formula “thus (چنین) said Rostam” with گفت functioning verbally (past tense). The following phrase برهام شیر is a direct address: برهام (Roham) is being addressed and شیر (“lion”) is an epithet/vocative applied to him. In classical Persian direct address often follows the verb of speaking or is set off with commas in prose; here the poetic order places them together after the reporting clause.
segments.s230-g128: This is Rostam’s quoted content. The first که introduces indirect speech (“that”); ترسم is the 1st-person present/future-of-fear “I fear.” The second که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. رخشم is رخش (face/countenance) + possessive -م “my face/countenance.” شد is the past/resultative “became” (here functioning like “has become”). از کار سیر is a prepositional phrase meaning “sated/tired of the affair” (از = from/because of, کار = the matter/affair, سیر = satiety/satisfied/weary). Altogether: “I fear that my face has become sated/weary because of the affair,” i.e. “I fear I am wearied by this business.”
segments.s230-g49: از ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (از + place). بیامد is a 3rd-person singular past-tense verb 'came'; in epic/poetic Persian the verb can remain singular even when the logical subject that follows is a plural noun. همی is an archaic/poetic preverbal particle here giving emphasis or a continuous/characteristic sense to the action. صد سوار is the (numeral + noun) subject 'a hundred horsemen' placed after the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s230-g50: This noun phrase describes those horsemen: زره‌دار is an adjective/noun 'armored (ones) / armored warrior'; با is the preposition 'with'; گرزه is the instrument 'mace'; گاوسار here functions as a name or epithet attached to the mace (the mace called Gāvsār). The whole phrase modifies or apposes the preceding 'hundred horsemen' (i.e. 'armored, with the mace Gāvsār').
segments.s230-g51: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). رستم is the grammatical subject 'Rostam'. بایرانیان here functions as the indirect object or addressee 'to the Iranians' (a dative/benefactive sense); classical/poetic word order places the verbphrase before the addressee.
segments.s230-g52: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that' / indirect command). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely / once and for all'. ببندند is 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive of بستن used to express a command or wish after که (literally 'that they should bind/close'); Persian uses this form for exhortation. کین is the direct object 'feud/vengeance' and is marked by the object marker را; میان ('between') follows the object as a postpositional complement (poetic order: 'bind the feud between').
segments.s230-g53: This is a list of oaths/invocations introduced elliptically: ب- prefixed to جان (بجان) functions like the English 'by' ('by the life'); سر شاه is 'the head of the king' (the ezāfe/genitive relation is implied without an explicit ezāfe in epic diction); و خورشید و ماه continues the list 'and the sun and the moon.' The whole string functions as the formulaic oath heading.
segments.s230-g54: This short clause is verbless (typical in high poetry); بخاک ('to the ground / into dust') + سیاوش ('Siavash') yields 'Siavash to the earth' (i.e. 'bury/throw Siavash into the ground'), and بایران سپاه supplies the agent/subject 'the Iranian host/army' (word order is inverted: object phrase precedes the subject). Because the verb is omitted, the sense relies on the preceding context (an oath or vow that the army will commit this act).
segments.s230-g55: This is an invocation/epithet to the divine. The three nouns are stacked as titles: بیزدان (an archaic/poetic epithet form addressing the divine), دادار 'the Creator' and جان‌آفرین 'life‑giver' — together functioning as a vocative or formulaic address. Such stacking is common in epic diction.
segments.s230-g56: که again introduces a subordinate clause ('that / so that / may it be that'). پیروزی is 'victory'. آورد is the 3rd-person singular past form of آوردن but here used in a hortative/wishful sense ('may [he/it] bring' / 'bring about'); classical Persian often uses this past form to express a desired result after an invocation. بر is the preposition 'on/over/against', and دشت کین is a genitive phrase 'the plain of vengeance' (دشت 'plain' + کین 'vengeance' as specification).
segments.s230-g89: که introduces a clause giving a reason or statement ('for' / 'that'). چون here means 'like/as' and introduces the comparison 'like him'. بگیتی is a prefixed locative (ب- + گیتی) meaning 'in the world'. سرافراز is an adjective 'lofty/exalted' placed before شاه (a common poetic word order), so the phrase reads 'a king exalted in the world like him'.
segments.s230-g90: نبود is past tense 'was not' (here negating the existence of such a king). و links the two predicates. ندیدست is an older/perhaps perfective negative form meaning 'had not seen' or 'was not seen' (poetic usage); it takes خورشید و ماه as its agents ('the sun and the moon had not seen [one like him]').
segments.s230-g91: شما را marks 'you' as the affected/experiencer (را topicalizes/dativizes). چه کارست is an idiomatic question 'what business is (it)' or 'what concern is it'; کارست is an archaic nominal + copula-like form meaning 'is the task/concern'. با introduces the object 'with a crown of gold' (تاج زر = golden crown, adjective زر postmodifies تاج).
segments.s230-g92: بدین is 'با/ب + دین' functioning as 'with this' or 'by this' (poetic/prepositional use of ب-). The phrase lists coordinated qualities (زور 'might/strength', این کوشش 'this effort', این هنر 'this skill'), with این repeated for emphasis before each noun; و simply coordinates them.
segments.s230-g93: همه دستها 'all hands/every hand' is the direct object/topic. سوی is a preposition 'toward/to'. آورید is an imperative (2nd-person plural) 'bring' or 'bring together' placed at the end, as is normal in Persian imperatives.
segments.s230-g94: میان را uses را to mark the place/target 'the middle' as definite. بخم is an archaic/obscure modifier (text-marked as archaic) modifying کمند; کمند is 'snare/lasso'. آورید is again imperative plural 'bring'. The sequence commands bringing the snare to the middle.
segments.s230-g95: شما را marks 'you' as the topical/affected party. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' ('from me' or 'in relation to me'). زندگانی 'life/livelihood' is the subject and بسست (archaic) means 'is enough' or 'sufficient' (copular -ست ending). The clause says that, with respect to me, your life is sufficient.
segments.s230-g96: که introduces a following explanatory clause ('that' / 'for'). تاج و نگین are coordinated nouns 'the crown and the gem/signet'. بهر means 'for' or 'intended for'. دیگر کسست uses کس (person/someone) with the copular -ست to state possession/assignment: 'are for another person.'
segments.s230-g105: This is an insulting nominal clause: تو ('thou') + سگزی (an epithet 'dog-like, base') followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('who/that'). از هر کسی بتری is a comparative construction using از + هر کسی ('than every/than anyone') + بتری (comparative 'worse'), so the whole segment reads 'you, a cur who is worse than anyone.' Word order is typical older/poetic Persian (subject/epithet before the relative clause).
segments.s230-g106: همی here is an emphatic/continuous adverb ('indeed/always'); شاه چین is a compound meaning 'king of Chin' (چین = China/Chin) used appositionally; بایدت is the personal-form of باید with enclitic -ت ('it is necessary for you' / 'you must'), and لشکری is the object 'troops/army'. The verb (بایدت) carries the necessity and the object لشکری follows it in this inverted poetic order: 'indeed, (for) you an army is necessary' = 'you need an army.'
segments.s230-g107: یکی here functions as an indefinite/meshing numeral 'a certain / a' (a single, intense instance), تیر باران is a compound noun meaning 'shower of arrows' (lit. 'arrow-rain'), and بکردند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they did/made'. سخت is adverbial 'strongly / fiercely'. The clause therefore = 'they made a fierce shower of arrows.'
segments.s230-g108: چو introduces a simile ('as / when'); باد خزان = 'the autumn wind' (باد = wind, خزان = autumn) and برجهد is a verb meaning 'leaps upon / strikes' (here 3rd-singular poetic form). بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and درخت is 'tree'. Together: 'as the autumn wind strikes (or shakes) the tree.'
segments.s230-g109: هوا را uses the definite object marker را after هوا ('the air') and puts the object before the verb for poetic effect. بپوشید is a past/preterite-form 'they covered' (verb with b- prefix), and پر عقاب is an instrumental/genitive phrase 'the feathers of eagles' or 'eagle-feathers' describing what filled the air. So: 'They covered the air with eagle-feathers.'
segments.s230-g110: نبیند is a negative-present/potential form of دیدن here rendered idiomatically as 'would not see' or 'does not see'; چنان ('such') modifies رزم جنگی ('a battle/warlike fight' — a pleonastic compound). بخواب is 'in sleep' / 'even in its sleep' (literally 'to sleep' used adverbially). The clause means 'such a battle an eagle would not see even in sleep,' i.e., even an eagle could not witness such a fight while asleep.
segments.s230-g111: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when.' گودرز is the subject (proper name), باران الماس is the object phrase 'a shower of diamonds' (baran used metaphorically), and دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw.' Word order here is subject — object — verb, normal for older Persian narrative.
segments.s230-g112: ز ('out of / from') marks cause ('out of concern for'). تیمار رستم = 'care/solicitude for Rostam' (تیمار with the following proper name). دلش is 'his heart' with enclitic ش ('his'), and بردمید is a poetic past verb meaning 'was carried off / was snatched' (i.e. his heart leapt/fled). The whole expresses an emotional reaction: 'from concern for Rostam, his heart was seized.'
segments.s230-g41: خروشی برآمد is a short nominal clause: the noun 'خروشی' (a shout/roar) comes before the verb 'برآمد' (arose/issued forth), a common poetic order. The prepositional phrase 'ز ایران سپاه' ('from Iran's host') uses the archaic/prepositional 'ز' = 'from' placed before 'ایران' with 'سپاه' in apposition to indicate the source of the shout.
segments.s230-g42: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'پیروز شد' is a compound meaning 'became victorious' (پیروز adjective + شد 'became'). 'گرد لشکر پناه' is a nominal phrase in which گرد functions as 'round/circle/about' (here 'round/circle of the army') and پناه 'refuge/protection' — poetically: when the army became victorious, the army's circle served as a shelter. The verb 'شد' governs the whole subordinate temporal clause; some linking words are omitted in this compressed poetic line.
segments.s230-g43: بفرمود رستم is an honorific narrative verb phrase: 'بفرمود' (he said/commanded) with the name 'رستم' following in normal subject position. The particle 'کز' here introduces the content/source clause and functions like 'that/from' (classical usage combining که + از): 'کز ایران سوار' = '(those) riders from Iran'. The subordinate clause supplies the origin and noun (سوار) while the finite verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s230-g44: The verb 'فرستند' (they sent) is 3rd plural past used to report the commanded action; the preposition 'بر' before the pronoun 'من' marks direction/recipient ('to me' — here 'بر' = 'toward/to'). 'صد نامدار' is the direct object 'a hundred renowned (men)' with the numeral before the adjective and noun; word order is the compact, poetic reverse of a fuller prose sentence.
segments.s230-g45: هم‌اکنون (right now) fronted for emphasis, followed by the subject 'من' (I). The phrase lists possessions without an explicit copular verb (classical/poetic omission), so possession is understood. Repeated 'آن' ('that') emphasizes each item; 'تخت عاج' = 'throne of ivory' (noun + material) is a compact attributive phrase common in poetry.
segments.s230-g46: The demonstrative 'همان' ('that very' / 'the same') introduces the next list of items. Items are coordinated with 'و' (and); 'یاره' here means an attendant/follower, and 'سنج' (scale) is another object. 'آن' again marks the following pair 'طوق و تاج' ('collar/necklace and crown'). All items are part of a nominal inventory — no finite verb is expressed in this line.
segments.s230-g47: ستانم is a 1st-person present/future-form verb 'I take/seize' (the -م ending marks 'I'); it governs the source phrase 'ز چین و بایران' ('from China and Bāyrān'), where two place-names are coordinated by 'و'. In this epic register the verb indicates intent/future action (I will seize). The last item 'بایران' appears here as a place-name and does not match the adjective senses listed for its lemma.
segments.s230-g48: This clause contains the recipients marked by the dative 'به' ('to/for'): 'به پیروز شاه' = 'to the victorious king.' The final noun 'دلیران' ('the brave ones/warriors') is a second beneficiary/recipient; the verb 'دهم' ('I give') is understood here (it was not listed among the segment words but completes the sentence), so the speaker says he will give (the taken goods) to the king and to the brave.
segments.s230-g9: ز نزدیک is a short prepositional phrase meaning 'from/at close range' (ز = poetic 'from'), چو here = 'as/when' introducing a clause, and بدید is a past-tense 3rd-person singular 'saw'. Word order is typical old/poetic Persian: circumstantial phrase (ز نزدیک) + temporal/comparative conjunction (چون) + object (ترگ رستم) + verb (بدید).
segments.s230-g10: یکی here is an indefinite quantifier 'a certain / one' (often rendered 'a' or 'suddenly' in poetry); باد سرد is the noun + adjective 'cold wind' (adj follows noun). از جگر is an ablative phrase 'from the liver/heart' used metaphorically for an inward, visceral feeling. برکشید is a simple past (3rd singular) meaning 'lifted/rose' (poetic past form). The whole clause describes an internal cold gust rising.
segments.s230-g11: بدل here functions adverbially 'in return / in reply', followed by گفت (used as speech/utterance). پیکار با ژنده پیل is the phrase 'battle with the ragged elephant' where پیکار is the object/what is being called for, با introduces the opponent, ژنده is an epithet 'ragged' and پیل is 'elephant'. Word order: [reply] + [said] + [command/noun phrase].
segments.s230-g12: چو begins a simile 'as / like', غوطه is a noun 'immersion/being submerged', and است is the copula 'is'. خوردن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'being eaten/consumed' (poetic use meaning 'to be swallowed'); بدریای نیل is a genitive phrase modifying that action ('of the Nile/sea of Nile' — here a grand, poetic image). Overall this phrase compares the scene to someone being swallowed by the Nile.
segments.s230-g13: گریزی is a verbal noun meaning 'an escape / a flight' or adjectival 'one who flees'; بهنگام is an adverb/adjective 'timely'. با سر بجای is a prepositional phrase 'with the head in its place' (i.e. with one's head still on) — با = 'with', سر = 'head', بجای = 'in place / remaining'. The phrase praises a timely retreat that preserves life.
segments.s230-g14: به از is a comparative construction 'better than' (literally 'to/for than'), followed by رزم جستن 'seeking battle' where رزم is 'battle' and جستن is the infinitive 'to seek/attempt'. بنام و برای are prepositional/phraseal motives 'for name (fame) and for [reward/aim]' — بنام = 'in the name of / for the sake of name', برای = 'for/for the purpose of'. Overall: 'better than seeking battle for name and gain.'
segments.s230-g15: گریزان is an adjective used substantively 'the fleeing one / fugitive', بیامد is past 'came', and سوی قلبگاه is a directional phrase 'toward the heart/core' (سوی = 'toward', قلبگاه = 'heart, center'). The subject is the fleeing person and the verb is simple past; the phrase marks motion toward the center.
segments.s230-g16: برو here functions as a motion verb 'went / went up' (imperative form can be used narratively in poetry), بر نظاره is a purpose/locative phrase 'onto a vantage for viewing' (بر = 'on/upon', نظاره = 'view/observation'), and ز هر سو سپاه is a phrase 'the host/army from every side' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every direction', سپاه = 'army'). Together: he moved up to survey the army on all sides.
segments.s230-g25: همی + past (خواست) produces an iterative/continuous past sense: 'he was intent/kept wishing.' تا introduces purpose/result ('in order that' / a subordinate clause), and کند is the present/subjunctive form 'do/make' used after تا to express the intended action. کارزاری is an adjectival/nominal compound 'of a campaign / a sortie' and is the object of کند.
segments.s230-g26: ندانست is the negative past 'he did not know / he did not understand' introducing a subordinate interrogative/indecision. کین here is an archaic demonstrative meaning 'this (occasion)' (none of the provided senses fit exactly). بار = 'occasion / time' and زاری is 'lamentation' with کند again as the potential/subjunctive 'to do / to make' (used to express whether he would perform the lament).
segments.s230-g27: چه نیکو بود is an exclamatory copular phrase 'how/what good it was'; بود is past/copular here giving the evaluative sense. هر که introduces a relative clause 'whoever / he who'; خود را is a reflexive object marked by the postposition را, and شناخت is used as the past verb 'knew/recognized (himself)'.
segments.s230-g28: چرا is a rhetorical 'why'; تا here introduces the subordinate clause of consequence/purpose ('that / so that'), and ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' ببایدش is a literary impersonal/obligative form 'it is necessary for him / he must' with the enclitic -ش = 'him,' and تاخت is a past action 'rushed/attacked' (here 'attacked' in context).
segments.s230-g29: پس is a temporal/consecutive 'then.' او is the pronoun 'him/he' (object here) and گرفته is a past participle 'having seized / having taken hold of.' گو introduces reported speech or an addressed utterance ('he said / cried'), and پیلتن is the vocative/name addressed. The sequence packs action plus speech: 'then, having seized him, he said to Piltan...'.
segments.s230-g30: که begins the content of what he said. هان is an interjection 'behold / hark / lo' calling attention. چاره is 'remedy / means.' The final phrase is compressed/poetic: گور and کفن are nouns 'grave' and 'shroud,' while کن‌گر supplies the verbal force (elliptically applying 'do/make' to those nouns) — effectively an imperative: 'dig a grave or make a shroud.' This ordering (nouns with a following verbal element) is a poetic compression.
segments.s230-g31: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone / a certain man.' نیزه is the direct object 'spear,' and زد is the simple past 'struck / thrust.' بر is the preposition 'at/on/upon' and کمربند is 'belt / girdle.' اوی is the archaic possessive/enclitic 'his' attached after the noun: 'his belt.' Word order follows older Persian patterns (verb after object or prepositional phrase).
segments.s230-g32: بدرید is the past 'they tore / cut apart.' The coordinated objects خفتان و پیوند اوی name the items damaged: خفتان is an archaic/poetic word (here meaning straps/fastenings — none of the provided senses match exactly), و is 'and,' پیوند is 'bond / fastening,' and اوی is the third‑person possessive 'his' attached to the second noun (so both items belong to him).
segments.s230-g113: برهام گفت is the reporting clause ('Borhām said'). ای is the vocative particle ('O'/'hey'), addressing someone; درنگی here names or characterizes the addressee as one who hesitates (a verbal-noun sense), and مایست is an archaic/poetic imperative meaning 'stand/stop' (used as a command). Together this introduces the quoted order.
segments.s230-g114: برو is the imperative 'go'. با introduces a comitative/instrumental phrase ('with' or 'by means of') followed by کمان 'bows'. وز here functions as an archaic connective linking items (effectively 'and' in older poetic usage), and سواری 'riders/horsemen' names the companions; دویست (end of the segment/sentence) is the numeral 'two hundred' quantifying them. The word order is imperative + prepositional phrase giving accompaniment/force.
segments.s230-g115: کمانهای is the plural 'bows' (the heads of the phrase). چاچی is an epithet or name qualifying the bows (here best treated as a proper/epithetic modifier), و is the coordinating 'and', and تیر خدنگ is a compound meaning 'arrows (tīr) of/like a khadang' (khadang = dagger/short-sword — i.e. deadly or heavy arrows). The phrase enumerates the missile weapons to be deployed.
segments.s230-g116: نگه‌دار can be read as a vocative noun 'guardian/keeper' (or an imperative 'keep/guard' in older usage), followed by پشت تهمتن 'the rear/back of Tahmatan' (پشت = 'rear/behind', تهمتن the hero's epithet), and بجنگ is an imperative 'fight'. Grammatically this is an instruction addressed to the guardians: guard the hero’s rear and engage in battle.
segments.s230-g117: بگیو is an archaic narrative/formulaic verb meaning roughly 'say!' or the cue 'he said', and آن زمان is the adverbial time phrase 'at that time/then'. گفت is the simple past 'said' (part of the reporting formula), and برکش سپاه is an imperative clause ('raise/lift up the host/army') with سپاه as the direct object. The sequence is the narrator introducing the command to muster the troops.
segments.s230-g118: برین دشت is a demonstrative locative phrase 'on this plain' (برین = 'this', دشت = 'plain'). زین بیش functions as 'beyond this / more than this' (زین = 'of/from/this', بیش = 'more'), and دشمن مخواه is a negative imperative 'do not seek/desire enemies' (مخواه = negative subjunctive/imperative 'do not ask/seek'). The whole clause warns not to look for additional foes on that field.
segments.s230-g119: نه ... است places the clause in negation: هنگام آرام و آسایش است literally 'it is a time of calm and ease', but with the initial نه this becomes 'it is not a time of calm and ease.' هنگام is the temporal noun 'time', آرام and آسایش are coordinated nouns meaning 'calm/peace' and 'comfort/ease', and است is the copula 'is'.
segments.s230-g120: The second half uses a parallel negation: نه نیز ('nor/also not') + از در رای و آرایش است. از در here means 'in the sphere of / in the matter of' (classical use), رای و آرایش pairs 'counsel/plan' and 'arrangement/array or ornament', and است is the copula. The whole line says it is not a time for counsel or for arranging/adornment either.
segments.s230-g73: هوا (subject) + گشت (past of گشتن) = 'the air became/turned'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. روی زنگی سیاه is a noun phrase 'the face of a Zangi, black' with adjective سیاه following the noun (typical Persian order). زنگی here means a dark-skinned person (poetic/ethnic epithet).
segments.s230-g74: ز (poetic from/of) + کشته (the slain, noun) is the source/possessive notion; ندیدند is 3rd person plural past negative 'they did not see'. بر دشت راه is a locative phrase 'on the plain road' (بر = on/upon). The whole clause means 'they saw none of the slain on the plain road.'
segments.s230-g75: همه (all/everywhere) starts the clause; مرز تن بود is a verbless-copular expression 'was a border/edge of bodies' (مرز = border/front). و خفتان و خود appends 'and sleepers and themselves'—خفتان is a poetic plural noun for 'those who sleep' (a euphemism for the dead), and خود here is emphatic 'the very selves/themselves.'
segments.s230-g76: تنان را uses را as the definite/object marker 'the bodies (as object)'; همی داد is the older epic imperfective/continuous past 'kept giving' (همی + داد). سرها is the subject 'the heads' and درود is the direct object 'greeting(s)'. Literal word order is object‑marker + verb + subject + object, common in poetry.
segments.s230-g77: ز + گرد سوار = 'from the dust of the riders' (گرد as 'dust' governing the genitive سوار). ابر is the noun 'cloud' and بر باد شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'was carried off/blown away by the wind' (بر باد شدن idiom); شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was'.
segments.s230-g78: زمین (the earth/ground) + پر (full) + ز (from/by) + آواز پولاد (the sound/clang of steel) + شد (became): a causative/resultive construction meaning 'the earth became full from the sound of steel.' پر z-construction marks what it is full of.
segments.s230-g79: بسی نامدار = 'many renowned' (بسی functions as an intensifier/quantifier). از پی is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of/after', taking the following nouns نام و ننگ ('name' = fame/reputation; ننگ = shame/disgrace) as the object of pursuit or motive. The phrase indicates motive/reason for their action.
segments.s230-g80: بدادند is 3rd person plural past 'they gave/they delivered' used idiomatically here to mean 'they engaged themselves' or 'they launched themselves'. بر + خیره سرها = 'against the headstrong/arrogant ones' (بر = against/atop), where خیره سرها is a plural nominal phrase meaning headstrong people. بجنگ is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to fight' used as the complement indicating the action they undertake ('they gave themselves to fight' = 'they fought').
segments.s230-g97: فرستم is a first‑person verb form meaning 'I send/dispatch' (an intentional sending). بنزدیک is an adverbial/locative meaning 'near, up to, close to'. شاه زمین is a genitive/noun phrase 'king of the earth' (literary expression for the supreme king). The whole segment means 'I will send (it) to the king of the earth.'
segments.s230-g98: The construction چه ... چه ... is used as a correlative 'whether ... or ... / whatever ... or ...'. منشور and شنگل are listed as items or insignia, joined by و 'and'. خاقان چین names a great ruler (Khaghan) qualified by چین 'China'. The segment enumerates the things to be sent: 'whether a manšūr and a shanggal, or the Khaghan of China.'
segments.s230-g99: و گرنه begins a conditional/contrast 'and if not / otherwise'. من is the first‑person subject 'I'. این خاک آوردگاه is a noun phrase where آوردگاه (literary) = 'place of bringing' used here as 'battlefield'; together 'this soil, this battlefield.' The phrase sets up the object/topic of what the speaker will act upon.
segments.s230-g100: بنعل ستوران is an adverbial/locative phrase literally 'under the horseshoe(s) of the steeds' (i.e., beneath the horses' hooves). برآرم is the first‑person singular present/future 'I will lift/raise up'. بماه literally 'to the moon' (a hyperbolic image meaning very high or utterly destroyed). Together: 'I will, under the horses' hooves, lift it up to the moon.'
segments.s230-g101: بدشنام ('the infamous one') names the addressee; بگشاد is a past form of گشودن 'opened' — here used with زبان 'tongue' to mean 'opened his mouth / began to speak'. خاقان is the subject 'the Khaghan'. The word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'the Khaghan opened (his) tongue to the infamous one.'
segments.s230-g102: بدو 'to him' marks the indirect object; گفت is the speech verb 'said' (here used as past). کای is the vocative 'O' addressing someone; بدتن and بدروان are epithets 'ill‑bodied' and 'ill‑faced / ill‑disposed'. The segment reports direct speech: 'he said to him, "O ill‑bodied, ill‑faced..."'.
segments.s230-g103: مه here is a poetic epithet 'moon' meaning 'ornament/splendor' (a common trope: 'the moon of X' = 'the glory of X'). ایران names the realm; the repeated مه with آن شاه و آن انجمن extends the image: 'I am the moon (pride) of Iran, (I am) the moon of that king and that assembly.' This is a self‑glorifying identification in parallel structure.
segments.s230-g104: همی is an adverb meaning 'ever/always' (emphatic). زینهاریت is a noun meaning 'your warning/admonition' (the -یت ending marks a noun of relation). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary', چو 'like/as', and من 'me/I'. The clause says 'your constant admonition must be like mine' or 'you must always take heed as I do.'
